POSIX FIFOs  
(Named Pipes)  
Inter-Process Communication Between  
Unrelated Processes

What is a FIFO?  
• FIFO = First In, First Out  
• A named pipe stored in the filesystem  
• Enables communication between unrelated processes  
• Works like a pipe, but has a path name (e.g., /tmp/myfifo)  
• Unidirectional by default (one-way)  
Process A  
Process B  
/tmp/myfifo  
Characteristics of FIFOs  
• Persistent object in filesystem until deleted  
• Created using mkfifo()  
• Accessed using open(), read(), write()  
• Data flows sequentially (byte stream)  
• Blocking behavior similar to pipes  
Creating a FIFO (mkfifo)  
• Prototype  
int mkfifo(const char *pathname, mode_t mode);  
• Example  
mkfifo("/tmp/myfifo", 0666);  
• Appears as a special file  
• Remains until manually removed (unlink())  
Opening a FIFO  
• Writer  
int fd = open("/tmp/myfifo", O_WRONLY);  
• Reader  
int fd = open("/tmp/myfifo", O_RDONLY);  
• open() blocks until the other side opens  
• Use O_NONBLOCK for non-blocking behavior  
FIFO Communication Flow  
Create FIFO using  
mkfifo()  
One process opens FIFO  
for read  
Another opens for write  
Use read() / write() call  
Close ends when done  
Remove FIFO with  
FIFO  
Reader  
Writer  
unlink()  
Example: FIFO Writer & Reader  
Writer blocks until a reader opens FIFO.  
int fd = open("/tmp/myfifo", O_WRONLY);  
write(fd, "Hello FIFO", 10);  
close(fd);  
int fd = open("/tmp/myfifo", O_RDONLY);  
read(fd, buffer, sizeof(buffer));  
close(fd);  
Blocking Behavior  
Reader gets 0 bytes (EOF)  
when:  
Writer blocks when:  
Reader blocks when:  
• No reader has opened the • No writer has opened the • All writers have closed the  
FIFO  
FIFO  
FIFO  
• FIFO empty (but writer still  
open)  
FIFO vs Anonymous Pipe  
FIFO  
Anonymous Pipe  
Feature  
Yes  
No  
No  
Has name  
Yes  
Visible in filesystem  
Works between  
Created by  
Any processes  
mkfifo()  
Until deleted  
One way  
Related processes only  
pipe()  
Until all ends closed  
One way  
Lifetime  
Direction  
Typical Use Cases  
• Communication between apps in different terminals  
• Producer/consumer programs  
• Logging processes  
• Simple command pipelines without shell  
• Embedded systems: sensor reader  
logger tool  
Example 1: FIFO Reader + Writer (1/4)  
• Instructions:  
o Terminal 1 → run reader program  
o Terminal 2 → run writer program  
o Observe blocking and output  
o Delete FIFO using unlink()  
• Expected output:  
Reader: Received → Hello FIFO  
Example 1: FIFO Reader + Writer (2/4)  
/* fifo_common.h – shared constants */  
#ifndef FIFO_COMMON_H  
#define FIFO_COMMON_H  
#define FIFO_PATH "/tmp/my_fifo" /* <-- change if you  
wish */  
#define MSG "Hello FIFO" /* message the writer sends */  
#endif /* FIFO_COMMON_H */  
/* Open FIFO for reading – this call blocks until a writer  
opens it */  
int fd = open(FIFO_PATH, O_RDONLY);  
if (fd == -1) {perror("open");exit(EXIT_FAILURE);}  
Example 1:  
(3/4)  
/* reader.c */  
char buf[256] = {0};  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <stdlib.h>  
#include <fcntl.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
#include <sys/stat.h>  
ssize_t n = read(fd, buf, sizeof(buf)-1);  
if (n == -1) {  
perror("read");  
close(fd);  
exit(EXIT_FAILURE);  
}
#include "fifo_common.h"  
int main(void){  
printf("Reader: Received → %s\n", buf);  
/* Create the FIFO if it does not exist yet */  
if (mkfifo(FIFO_PATH, 0666) == -1) {  
close(fd);  
perror("mkfifo");  
/* Remove the FIFO – optional; you can also do it from a  
/* If the FIFO already exists we can ignore  
third program */  
if (unlink(FIFO_PATH) == -1) {  
perror("unlink");  
}
the error */  
}
return 0;  
}
Example 1:  
(4/4)  
/* writer.c */  
ssize_t w = write(fd, MSG, sizeof(MSG)); /*  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <stdlib.h>  
#include <fcntl.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
include terminating '\0' */  
if (w == -1) {  
perror("write");  
close(fd);  
#include "fifo_common.h"  
exit(EXIT_FAILURE);  
}
int main(void)  
{
printf("Writer: Sent → %s\n", MSG);  
/* Open FIFO for writing – this will block until a  
close(fd);  
return 0;  
}
reader has opened it */  
int fd = open(FIFO_PATH, O_WRONLY);  
if (fd == -1) {  
perror("open");  
exit(EXIT_FAILURE);  
}
Example 2: Multi-Message FIFO (1/6)  
• Task:  
• Writer sends multiple sensor values  
• Reader prints each with timestamp  
• Stop when writer closes FIFO  
• Learning: EOF + streaming behavior  
Example 2: Multi-Message FIFO (2/6)  
/* fifo_common.h – shared constants */  
#ifndef FIFO_COMMON_H  
#define FIFO_COMMON_H  
#define FIFO_PATH "/tmp/sensor_fifo" /* name of the FIFO */  
#define NUM_VALUES 10 /* how many values the writer sends */  
#define SLEEP_SEC 1 /* delay between two values (seconds) */  
#endif /* FIFO_COMMON_H */  
Example 2: Multi-Message FIFO (3/6)  
/* reader.c ------------------------------------------  
------------------ */  
static void print_with_timestamp(const char *value)  
{
#include <stdio.h>  
time_t now = time(NULL);  
struct tm tm_now;  
#include <stdlib.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
localtime_r(&now, &tm_now);  
char ts[32];  
#include <fcntl.h>  
#include <time.h>  
strftime(ts, sizeof(ts), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S",  
&tm_now);  
#include <string.h>  
#include "fifo_common.h"  
printf("Reader [%s] → %s\n", ts, value);  
fflush(stdout);  
#define LINE_MAX 128  
}
/* maximum length of a line from the FIFO */  
char line[LINE_MAX];  
while (fgets(line, sizeof(line), fp) != NULL) {  
/* Strip trailing newline */  
line[strcspn(line, "\n")] = '\0';  
print_with_timestamp(line);  
}
Example 2: (4/6)  
int main(void)  
{
/* Open FIFO for reading – blocks until writer  
/* fgets() returned NULL – either error or EOF (writer  
opens it */  
closed) */  
int fd = open(FIFO_PATH, O_RDONLY);  
if (feof(fp))  
if (fd == -1) {  
perror("open");  
exit(EXIT_FAILURE);  
}
printf("Reader: EOF reached – writer has closed  
the FIFO.\n");  
else  
perror("fgets");  
/* Wrap the fd into a FILE* so we can use fgets()  
fclose(fp); /* also closes the underlying fd */  
/* Optional clean-up of the FIFO (remove the file) */  
if (unlink(FIFO_PATH) == -1)  
(line-oriented I/O) */  
FILE *fp = fdopen(fd, "r");  
if (!fp) {  
perror("unlink");  
perror("fdopen");  
close(fd);  
return 0;  
}
exit(EXIT_FAILURE);  
}
Example 2: Multi-Message FIFO (5/6)  
/* writer.c -------------------------------------------  
----------------- */  
/* Open the FIFO for writing – blocks until a reader  
opens it */  
#include <stdio.h>  
int fd = open(FIFO_PATH, O_WRONLY);  
#include <stdlib.h>  
if (fd == -1) {  
perror("open");  
exit(EXIT_FAILURE);  
}
#include <unistd.h>  
#include <fcntl.h>  
#include <time.h>  
#include "fifo_common.h"  
int main(void)  
{
/* Create the FIFO if it does not exist yet */  
if (mkfifo(FIFO_PATH, 0666) == -1) {  
perror("mkfifo");  
/* If the FIFO already exists we can ignore the  
error */  
}
Example 2: Multi-Message FIFO (6/6)  
/* Stream NUM_VALUES random sensor readings, one per line */  
srand((unsigned)time(NULL));  
for (int i = 0; i < NUM_VALUES; ++i) {  
int sensor = rand() % 1000; /* dummy sensor value */  
dprintf(fd, "%d\n", sensor); /* write value + newline */  
printf("Writer: sent value %d\n", sensor);  
fflush(stdout);  
sleep(SLEEP_SEC);  
}
/* Closing the descriptor sends EOF to the reader */  
close(fd);  
printf("Writer: finished, FIFO closed → EOF will be seen by  
reader.\n");  
return 0;  
}
Example 3: Chat Application using FIFO  
• Task:  
• Create two FIFOs: fifo1, fifo2  
• Terminal 1 reads from fifo1, writes to fifo2  
• Terminal 2 does the opposite  
• Build a simple two-terminal chat  
Common Pitfalls  
• Forgetting to create FIFO before opening  
• Opening write-only FIFO → blocks forever  
• Not removing FIFO after use  
• Using FIFO for large binary blobs (better use sockets/shared memory)  
• Multiple writers → interleaving data  
Troubleshooting  
• Use ls -l to verify FIFO exists  
• Use strace to debug blocking behavior  
• Check open() flags  
• Ensure permissions allow read/write  
• Use unlink() to clean old FIFOs  
Summary  
• FIFOs are named pipes for process-to-process communication  
• Work between unrelated processes  
• Blocking/open rules must be understood  
• Great for small IPC and teaching IPC concepts  
• Simple, efficient alternative to sockets or message queues  
Scenario: Multi-Terminal Command Dispatcher  
(1/3)  
Context  
• Create a command dispatcher that allows any terminal to send commands to a central daemon via a  
named pipe.  
• The daemon executes simple actions and returns text responses over per-client response FIFOs.  
Scenario: Multi-Terminal Command Dispatcher  
(2/3)  
PART 1 — Daemon (Dispatcher)  
• On startup: create main FIFO /tmp/cmd_fifo.  
• Loop: open for read, receive commands of form:  
<client_fifo_name> <command> [args...]  
• Supported commands: echo <text> → respond with <text>  
• time → respond with current time  
• rev <text> → respond with reversed text  
• Open the provided <client_fifo_name> for write and send response.  
• Robustness: ignore malformed messages; keep running.  
Scenario: Multi-Terminal Command Dispatcher  
(3/3)  
PART 2 — Client Tool  
• Create a unique response FIFO, e.g., /tmp/resp_<pid>.  
• Write request to /tmp/cmd_fifo with your response FIFO path.  
• Open your response FIFO for read; print response; unlink it.
